nativeRange.habitat: Coral reef crevices at depths of approximately 8–30 m, favoring sheltered microhabitats within rockwork where light intensity is low.

diet.notes: In the wild, this species is believed to feed on small invertebrates and crustaceans — a dietary profile consistent with its trophic level. In captivity, it has been documented accepting vitamin-enriched frozen mysis shrimp and brine shrimp, and is generally considered easy to feed once it has settled into the aquarium.

imageCandidates.notes: Tristan Lougher; 640x340. Sharp, well-framed live individual on reef rubble showing the diagnostic dark vertical bars, yellow head striping and bright green eye. Only true natural-pose live shot found; low resolution is the sole caveat.
P.C. Heemstra / SAIAB; 598x400. Fresh specimen in a blue photo-tank, in focus, lateral view clearly showing the black vertical body bars; body colour slightly washed.
Daniel Pelicier / SAIAB; 600x540. Fresh specimen in a blue photo-tank showing black bars and reddish body with erect fins; slightly oblique angle.

summary: The Black Barred Convict Goby is a small, cryptic reef fish from the Indo-Pacific that tends to spend daylight hours sheltered within rockwork and coral crevices, emerging more actively after dark. It is generally considered reef-safe and peaceful, making it a quiet addition to a community reef aquarium.
careNotes:
The Black Barred Convict Goby takes its species name from its presumed nocturnal habits — 'nocturna' — and tends to spend much of the day resting within rockwork crevices, becoming more active once the aquarium lights go out. A tank with ample hiding places, including caves, overhangs, and layered live rock, is generally advised to help the fish feel secure and settle into a regular feeding routine.

Because this species benefits from stable reef chemistry and an established population of live rock, a tank of at least 75 litres is recommended as a practical minimum for long-term keeping.

Escape risk: gobies of this genus have been known to jump from open-top aquariums, so a well-fitted cover is generally advised.

No captive breeding information has been documented for this species.

additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name (confirmed Priolepis nocturna), native range (Indo-Pacific: Seychelles/Maldives to Marquesas, Philippines, Phoenix Islands), size (3.9 cm SL), temperature (26.2–28.9°C wild habitat — disagrees with LiveAquaria's 22.2–25.6°C; averaged range 24–27°C applied), taxonomy (Gobiidae/Gobiiformes), and diet (trophic level 3.2, carnivore).
